us-politics.headline: Iran Rejects US Proposal as Troop Deployment Talk Grows
us-politics.body:
Iran called the US peace proposal 'unrealistic' and rejected it outright, even as Trump described a separate Iranian ceasefire offer as 'significant' but 'not good enough.' Israel struck an Iranian petrochemical plant ahead of Trump's self-imposed deadline, raising the temperature further. Trump held a press conference where he highlighted a US officer's rescue operation inside Iran, signaling active engagement on the ground.

At least one House Republican now predicts US troop deployment is inevitable. JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon warned a prolonged conflict could tip the economy into recession. Views split sharply on military options: one senior GOP defense voice argues the US can reopen the Strait of Hormuz without a ground invasion, while critics warn Iran can win simply by not losing - drawing comparisons to Vietnam, Afghanistan, and Iraq.

sources.title: Live updates: Trump details officer’s ‘historic’ rescue in Iran at press conference
Iran could defeat the US just by not losing
House Republican predicts Trump will deploy troops in Iran: ‘I just don’t see any other way’
Trump: Iran ceasefire proposal ‘significant,’ ‘not good enough’
Top GOP defense voice breaks with invasion fears, says US can reopen Hormuz without ground war
Trump reveals Iran made 'significant proposal' after ultimatum, but 'not good enough'
Israel hits petrochemical plant in Iran ahead of Trump deadline
Dimon warns Iran war could sink economy into a recession
Iran spurns ‘unrealistic’ US peace proposal

us-politics.headline: Supreme Court Clears Path to Dismiss Bannon Conviction
us-politics.body:
The Supreme Court sided with Steve Bannon, clearing the way for his contempt of Congress conviction to be dismissed following Trump's pardon. The court also cleared a parallel case involving a former Cincinnati city councilman pardoned by Trump, and returned a separate state-secrets privilege case to a lower court.

The Bannon ruling removes a conviction stemming from his refusal to cooperate with the January 6 committee. Critics argue the decision sets a damaging precedent for congressional oversight. Supporters say it correctly applies pardon power. The rulings collectively mark a significant week for Trump-aligned defendants in federal courts.

sources.title: Supreme Court sides with Steve Bannon in bid to dismiss Jan. 6 conviction
Supreme Court returns state-secrets privilege case to lower court
Supreme Court clears the way for Bannon contempt case to be dismissed
Supreme Court clears way for dismissal in case of ex-Cincinnati City Council member pardoned by Trump
Supreme Court paves way for dismissal of Steve Bannon contempt of Congress case

us-politics.headline: Democrats Struggle to Rebuild as Republicans Eye State Seats
us-politics.body:
Democrats announced their first slate of state legislative targets ahead of November, focusing on flipping GOP-held seats in key states. The move signals an early ground-level push after a bruising election cycle. But analysts warn the party faces deeper structural problems that Republican stumbles alone will not fix.

Separately, commentary across the political spectrum points to Democrats losing ground in the media landscape, with MAGA-aligned podcasters commanding audiences that traditional Democratic outreach is failing to reach. The party's path back, several observers argue, runs through local politics and new media - not just opposition to Trump.

sources.title: Democrats have too many troubles to cheer Trump’s failures
Democrats announce first slate of state legislative targets ahead of November
Democrats face new reality: Podcasters’ political power

us-politics.headline: OpenAI Proposes AI Tax Framework; Tesla Probe Closed
us-politics.body:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman released a policy blueprint calling for a federal framework to tax and regulate artificial intelligence, positioning the company ahead of expected congressional debate on AI governance. The proposal advocates for a light-touch regulatory approach while acknowledging risks from unchecked AI development.

In a separate tech development, federal regulators closed their investigation into Tesla's 'Actually Smart Summon' feature, finding insufficient evidence to mandate a recall. The closure is a win for Tesla, which has faced repeated scrutiny over its driver-assistance systems. Both stories reflect a broader tension in Washington over how aggressively to regulate fast-moving technology.

sources.title: OpenAI’s Altman releases blueprint for taxing, regulating artificial intelligence
Regulator closes investigation into Tesla ‘actually smart summon’ feature

au-politics.headline: Iran Rejects Ceasefire as Chinese AI Sharpens Its Targeting
au-politics.body:
Iran has rejected a 45-day ceasefire proposal as Trump prepares to address the ongoing conflict, with diplomatic off-ramps narrowing fast. Separately, reports indicate Chinese satellite intelligence is helping Iranian forces target US military assets with greater precision, raising the stakes for any negotiated pause.

The combination of a collapsing ceasefire window and upgraded Iranian targeting capability signals a conflict growing harder to contain. Australia has not yet publicly responded to the Chinese intelligence allegations, though the claims carry direct implications for regional allies and US force protection in the Middle East.

sources.title: Live: Trump due to speak as Iran rejects 45-day ceasefire proposal
Chinese AI helping Iran target US forces with 'incredible precision'

au-politics.headline: AI in Government: Scientists Warn of Robodebt-Style Risk
au-politics.body:
Scientists are warning that using AI to prepare and evaluate environmental assessments could repeat the failures of the robodebt scheme, where automated systems produced flawed outcomes at scale with little human oversight. The concern centres on governments outsourcing complex judgements to tools that cannot account for ecological nuance or legal accountability.

The Spectator has separately criticised the Albanese government's expansion of free online mental health services as bureaucratic overreach, arguing that government-run digital therapy creates dependency rather than solving underlying problems. Both debates reflect a broader tension over where automated or state-delivered services help citizens and where they cause harm.

sources.title: Using AI to prepare and evaluate environmental assessments risks ‘robodebt-style’ failures, scientists say
Free online therapy from a headache-inducing government

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites: Outback Pubs Empty, City Commuters Cycling
au-politics.body:
Rising fuel costs are reshaping how Australians move. In Sydney, commuters are switching to bikes in growing numbers to avoid what some describe as 'crazy' petrol prices. In outback Queensland, the Pink Roadhouse - a landmark tourist stop - recorded just two visitors over Easter, with the owner linking the collapse in traffic directly to fuel costs making long drives unaffordable.

The two stories together paint a picture of a cost-of-living squeeze hitting both urban and regional Australians, though in very different ways. City dwellers have alternatives. Remote communities and tourism-dependent businesses do not.

sources.title: Cars make way for bikes as Sydney commuters saddle up to circumvent ‘crazy’ fuel costs
Outback tourist pub had two visitors this Easter amid fuel price pinch

au-politics.headline: Cyclone Maila Threatens Queensland Weeks After Narelle
au-politics.body:
Far north Queensland is bracing for Severe Tropical Cyclone Maila, just three weeks after Cyclone Narelle caused significant damage to the same region. Emergency services and communities already stretched by the earlier storm now face a second major weather event with limited recovery time.

A toddler also died after being pulled from water on a Queensland property over the Easter period, adding to a difficult week for the state. The back-to-back cyclone threat will test both emergency infrastructure and the resilience of affected communities across the far north.

sources.title: Toddler dies after being pulled from water on Queensland property
Severe Tropical Cyclone Maila could hit far north Queensland three weeks after Narelle tore through

world.headline: US-Iran conflict escalates across military, diplomatic and energy fronts
world.body:
The United States and Iran are locked in an accelerating confrontation that now spans strikes, covert operations and economic warfare. US forces rescued a downed air officer from a remote part of Iran in a covert extraction, while Trump has publicly threatened to destroy Iranian power plants, pharmaceutical facilities and civilian bridges - raising alarm over civilian casualties. Iran has responded by threatening to close the Bab al-Mandeb strait, a chokepoint carrying roughly 10% of global trade.

Pakistan has stepped in with a two-phased truce proposal, but Iran has rejected any temporary ceasefire that leaves Hormuz off the table. Questions are also mounting over whether Trump inadvertently confirmed Iranian claims that US-armed protesters were operating inside the country. The crisis is rippling outward - Libya's oil disputes and Hormuz tensions are now feeding European energy fears simultaneously.

sources.title: Where are Iran’s power plants that Trump has threatened to destroy?
In maps and photos: Five Iranian civilian bridges at risk of US strikes
Bombing of Iran's pharma producers threatens patients' lives
How US operation to rescue air officer from Iran unfolded
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Has Trump confirmed Iran’s claim that protesters were US-armed?
Iran threatens Bab al-Mandeb closure: How would that affect world trade?
Pakistan offers ‘two-phased’ truce deal to end US-Israel war on Iran
Trump’s ‘Bridge Day’ threat: Can a last-ditch ceasefire plan work?
Libya’s oil disputes mirror Hormuz crisis, fuel European energy fears

world.headline: Israel expands war on multiple fronts, killing and displacing millions
world.body:
Israel is fighting simultaneously in Gaza and Lebanon, with mounting civilian costs on both fronts. In Gaza, Israeli army fire struck a WHO vehicle, killing one aid worker, while a separate report documents a grandmother who lost her entire family to Israeli strikes. Young Palestinians face mass unemployment as Israel maintains a stranglehold on Gaza's economy.

In Lebanon, Israeli strikes have destroyed towns in the south and hit areas around Beirut previously designated as safe zones, forcing up to 1.2 million people to flee. The dual campaigns are drawing sustained international criticism, with aid access in Gaza remaining severely restricted and Lebanese displacement reaching levels not seen since the 2006 war.

sources.title: Israeli army fire on WHO vehicle in southern Gaza kills one, medics report
Gaza grandmother loses her family due to Israeli attacks
Jobless young Palestinians trapped as Israel holds Gaza’s economy hostage
Israel destroys southern Lebanon towns, hits ‘safe’ areas around Beirut
Up to 1.2 million people forced to flee as Israel pummels Lebanon
Gaza camp offers children a sense of safety

world.headline: Ukraine hits Russian energy infrastructure deep inside Russian territory
world.body:
Ukraine struck the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk, one of Russia's most significant energy export hubs, in a bold long-range operation. Separately, Ukrainian attacks on Russian oil refineries are now being felt inside Russia itself - residents of St Petersburg are reportedly smelling smoke as refinery strikes edge closer to major population centres.

The campaign marks a deliberate Ukrainian strategy to degrade Russian energy revenue and logistics. Novorossiysk handles a substantial share of Russian oil exports, and sustained damage there could tighten global oil supply at a moment when multiple other energy flashpoints - Libya, Hormuz, Bab al-Mandeb - are already unsettling markets.

sources.title: Ukraine strikes Russian Black Sea energy hub Novorossiysk
‘Smell’ of war comes to St Petersburg as Ukraine hammers Russian refineries

world.headline: Myanmar junta chief takes presidency; Kim's daughter emerges as heir
world.body:
Myanmar's military chief Min Aung Hlaing has been formally elected president, consolidating his grip on power more than four years after the coup that toppled Aung San Suu Kyi's government. The move raises questions about whether the junta will now seek international legitimacy or press harder against resistance forces still active across the country.

Meanwhile, South Korean intelligence now assesses that Kim Jong Un's daughter, Ju Ae, is the most likely successor to lead North Korea. She has appeared with increasing frequency at high-profile military events, and analysts say the signals from Pyongyang point clearly toward a dynastic handover being prepared.

sources.title: Myanmar junta chief elected president — what comes next?
North Korea: Kim's daughter now seen as likely heir — South

ai.headline: OpenAI Outlines Robot Taxes, Four-Day Week for AI Era
ai.body:
OpenAI has published a policy vision calling for public wealth funds, taxes on robots, and a four-day work week as automation reshapes labor markets. The proposals acknowledge that AI will displace workers at scale and argue governments need structural tools to redistribute the gains. It is a notable shift for a company that has largely avoided prescribing policy.

The timing matters. MIT Technology Review reports that granular occupational data - tracking exactly which tasks AI handles versus humans - remains scarce, making it hard to measure displacement in real time. Without that data, any policy response is flying blind. OpenAI's proposals are bold on vision but arrive ahead of the evidence needed to calibrate them.

sources.title: OpenAI’s vision for the AI economy: public wealth funds, robot taxes, and a four-day work week
The one piece of data that could actually shed light on your job and AI

ai.headline: AI Floods iTunes Chart, Propaganda Concerns Mount
ai.body:
An AI-generated singer called Eddie Dalton now holds eleven spots on the iTunes singles chart simultaneously, despite having no human identity behind the name. The chart takeover is a clean demonstration of how AI can game distribution systems built for human artists.

The broader pattern is harder to ignore. A Time analysis argues that virality itself has become the payload in AI-driven propaganda - content engineered not to persuade through argument but to spread through sheer volume and algorithmic momentum. Together, these stories point to the same problem: platforms designed around human-scale content creation are structurally unprepared for machine-scale output.

sources.title: AI Singer Now Occupies Eleven Spots on iTunes Singles Chart
When Virality Is the Message: The New Age of AI Propaganda

ai.headline: Claude Code Backlash, Freestyle Sandboxes Launch for Agents
ai.body:
A widely-shared GitHub issue accuses Anthropic's Claude Code of becoming unusable for complex engineering work following February updates, with developers reporting degraded reasoning and broken multi-step tasks. The thread has drawn significant attention from the AI coding community and puts pressure on Anthropic to respond publicly.

Into that gap steps Freestyle, a new startup launching sandboxed execution environments built specifically for AI coding agents. The pitch is isolation and safety - letting agents run code without touching production systems. Reducto also released Deep Extract, an agent-based document parsing tool aimed at enterprise data pipelines. The tooling layer around AI agents is maturing fast, even as the underlying models draw complaints.

sources.title: Claude Code is unusable for complex engineering tasks with the Feb updates
Launch HN: Freestyle: Sandboxes for AI Coding Agents
Reducto releases Deep Extract

ai.headline: Spain's Xoople Raises $130M to Feed AI Earth Data
ai.body:
Barcelona-based Xoople has closed a $130 million Series B to build high-resolution geospatial datasets for AI training and inference. The company maps physical infrastructure, land use, and environmental change at a scale that satellite imagery alone cannot match. Demand is coming from logistics, climate modeling, and autonomous systems.

The raise reflects a broader bet that AI's next constraint is not compute or model architecture but grounded, real-world data. Intel is making a parallel wager - Wired reports the chipmaker is doubling down on advanced chip packaging technology, arguing that how chips connect physically will determine who wins the next phase of AI hardware. Both stories point to the same thesis: the infrastructure beneath AI matters as much as the models on top.

sources.title: Spain’s Xoople raises $130 million Series B to map the Earth for AI
The Ridiculously Nerdy Intel Bet That Could Rake in Billions

btc.headline: Bitcoin Hovers Near $70K as Iran Tensions Drive Volatility
btc.body:
Bitcoin climbed above $70,000 Monday before profit-taking pulled it back, with geopolitical noise doing most of the driving. Trump's ultimatum to Iran rattled risk assets early, then a report of ceasefire talks sparked a quick rally - illustrating how tightly BTC is tracking macro headlines right now.

Analysts see contrarian bottoming signals building, but the path to $75,000 depends heavily on whether the Iran situation escalates or cools. Prediction markets are functioning as real-time macro radar, with Iran war bets moving in lockstep with crypto prices. The week ahead also brings inflation data, adding another potential catalyst for sharp moves in either direction.

sources.title: Can Trump’s ultimatum to Iran push Bitcoin price back up to $75K?
Bitcoin climbs above $70,000 as more contrarian bottoming signs emerge
Bitcoin profit taking keeps BTC below $70K as Trump doubles down on Iran
Bitcoin has room to rally, but there's a catch
Bitcoin rallies on report of Iran ceasefire talks, Algorand extends gains
First real bull signal since 2025? Five things to know in Bitcoin this week
Iran war bets turn prediction markets into real-time macro radar: Sygnum
Inflation takes center stage: Crypto Week Ahead

btc.headline: Strategy Buys More Bitcoin While Tokenization Divides Wall Street
btc.body:
Strategy added 4,871 BTC for $330 million last week, pushing holdings near 767,000 BTC despite paper losses exceeding $14.5 billion in Q1. The firm is not blinking. Meanwhile, Bitmine's Ethereum treasury hit 4.8 million ETH as it prepares to list on the NYSE, signaling that corporate crypto treasury strategies are spreading beyond Bitcoin.

On the institutional side, Jamie Dimon told JPMorgan it needs to move faster as tokenization reshapes finance - a striking admission from a longtime crypto skeptic. The IMF pushed back, warning that tokenization could import crypto's volatility and risks into mainstream financial markets. Bernstein analysts argued the opposite for Figure Technology, saying its tokenized credit platform could double the company's stock price.

sources.title: Figure Technology's tokenized credit platform could help double stock price: Bernstein
Jamie Dimon says JPMorgan must move faster as tokenization reshapes finance
Strategy adds $330M BTC as paper losses top $14.5B in Q1
Bitmine's ether treasury hits 4.8 million ETH as stock listing moves to NYSE
Strategy added another 4,871 bitcoin for $330 million, with holdings nearing 767,000 BTC
IMF warns tokenization could bring crypto risks into global financial markets

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Score Legal Win, Upgrade Infrastructure
btc.body:
Kalshi won a key legal battle Monday as a federal appeals court blocked New Jersey from shutting down its sports prediction markets. The ruling is a significant precedent for the sector's ability to operate across state lines without regulatory interference.

Polymarket separately announced a full exchange upgrade that hands the platform direct control over its own trading and market resolution - reducing reliance on third-party infrastructure. Together, the moves suggest prediction markets are maturing fast, both legally and technically, just as geopolitical events like the Iran standoff are driving record engagement on these platforms.

sources.title: Polymarket reveals a 'full exchange upgrade' to take control of its own trading and truth
Appeals court blocks New Jersey from shutting down Kalshi's sports markets
Iran war bets turn prediction markets into real-time macro radar: Sygnum

btc.headline: Quantum Threats Split Crypto Community on How to Respond
btc.body:
Circle launched its Arc blockchain with built-in quantum-resistant cryptography, betting that future-proofing now is cheaper than retrofitting later. The move puts Circle ahead of most of the industry on a threat that remains theoretical but is advancing faster than many expected.

Not everyone thinks speed is the answer. Samson Mow warned that a rushed quantum fix for Bitcoin could backfire, introducing new vulnerabilities in the scramble to patch old ones. The debate reflects a genuine tension - waiting too long is dangerous, but moving too fast on a network securing hundreds of billions of dollars carries its own serious risks.

sources.title: Rushed quantum fix may backfire for Bitcoin, Samson Mow warns
Circle future-proofs Arc blockchain against quantum computing threats

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky inside with a caramelised sweet-savoury miso crust
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before roasting — it lets the glaze sink in and speeds up cooking by about five minutes

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear corridor or outdoor path
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries (one side only), and overhead carries for 40-metre lengths, resting 60 seconds between sets — builds grip, core stability, and real-world strength in a single unglamorous session

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — philosophers from Hobbes to Parfit have extended this to human cells, memories, and selfhood
learn.why: On a Monday at the start of a new week it is a quietly unsettling question: the person who made last week's mistakes has been partially replaced at the cellular level — and Derek Parfit's 1984 book Reasons and Persons takes the rabbit hole somewhere genuinely strange about what personal continuity even means
